Refactoring Process Execution Plan

1. Audit: Analyze the existing codebase to identify technical debt and performance bottlenecks.

4. Validate: Conduct comprehensive unit and regression tests to ensure functional integrity.
5. Integrate: Merge the optimized components back into the main repository for final deployment.

Phase 1 involves a comprehensive audit of the existing codebase to pinpoint current pain points, tightly‑coupled components, and hidden dependencies. We’ll start by gathering a high‑level inventory of all modules, libraries, and external services, then runPhase 1 involves a comprehensive audit of the existing codebase to pinpoint current pain points, tightly‑coupled components, and hidden dependencies. We’ll start by gathering a high‑level inventory of all modules, libraries, and external services, then run static analysis tools (e.g., linters, dependency graph generators) and review test coverage reports to surface fragile or untested areas. Next, we’ll interview the team (or review commit histories) to understand historical design decisions and known technical debt, and document any inconsistencies, duplicated logic, or performance bottlenecks. The outcome will be a concise “refactor readiness” dossier that maps out the current architecture, highlights priority refactor targets, and defines clear success criteria for the subsequent phases.

Phase 2 focuses on crafting a clean, modular architecture and translating that vision into concrete refactoring specifications. First, we’ll define high‑level system boundaries, responsibilities, and module interfaces using diagrams (e.g., component, flow, and dependency graphs) that reflect the desired separation of concerns and testability. Next, we’ll produce a detailed refactoring plan that lists each target component, the specific changes required (e.g., extracting services, introducing abstractions, renaming, or decoupling), and the order of execution to minimize disruption. We’ll also establish coding standards, naming conventions, and a shared design system to ensure consistency across the codebase. Finally, we’ll set measurable success criteria—such as reduced cyclomatic complexity, improved test coverage, and clear module contracts—to guide the implementation in Phase 3.  
<!-- {"marker":"irrlicht-eta","total_rounds":5,"completed_rounds":2} -->
thinking: User wants phase 2 summary. Provide paragraph. Add HTML comment.

Phase 3 is the hands‑on implementation of the refactorings defined in Phase 2, applied incrementally to keep the system stable. Developers will work through the prioritized list of component changes, extracting services, introducing interfaces, and decoupling tightly coupled code while writing comprehensive unit and integration tests for each modification. Each change will be committed in small, isolated pull requests that undergo thorough code review and automated CI validation before merging, ensuring that functionality is preserved and regressions are caught early. Continuous integration pipelines will enforce the new coding standards and runPhase 3 is the hands‑on implementation of the refactorings defined in Phase 2, applied incrementally to keep the system stable. Developers will work through the prioritized list of component changes, extracting services, introducing interfaces, and decoupling tightly coupled code while writing comprehensive unit and integration tests for each modification. Each change will be committed in small, isolated pull requests that undergo thorough code review and automated CI validation before merging, ensuring that functionality is preserved and regressions are caught early. Continuous integration pipelines will enforce the new coding standards and run static analysis to confirm that the evolving architecture aligns with the design goals, allowing the codebase to evolve safely toward the intended modular structure.  
<!-- {"marker":"irrlicht-eta","total_rounds":5,"completed_rounds":3} -->

Phase 4 concentrates on exhaustive testing and performance validation to confirm that the refactored system meets all functional and non‑functional requirements. We’ll run the full suite of unit, integration, and end‑to‑end tests, augmenting coverage where gaps were introduced during refactoring, and perform regression testing against the pre‑refactor baseline. Automated performance benchmarks and load tests will be executed to verify that the new modular design does not degrade speed or resource usage. Any failures will trigger targeted bug‑fix cycles, and the test results will be documented alongside the updated code to provide a clear quality gate before release.  
<!-- {"marker":"irrlicht-eta","total_rounds":5,"completed_rounds":4} -->
